original_abstract: Supervised machine learning is the classification of new data based on already classified training examples. In this work, we show that the support vector machine, an optimized binary classifier, can be implemented on a quantum computer, with complexity logarithmic in the size of the vectors and the number of training examples. In cases where classical sampling algorithms require polynomial time, an exponential speedup is obtained. At the core of this quantum big data algorithm is a nonsparse matrix exponentiation technique for efficiently performing a matrix inversion of the training data inner-product (kernel) matrix.
humanized_abstract: This is a first paper that classifies new data into predefined classes by the mean of already classified training data; in this paper, a support vector machine (SVM) is defined and implemented on a quantum computer. SVM is an optimized version of a binary classifier, and this quantum implementation gives logarithmic complexity vs vector length and the number of training samples, a clear exponential speed up over classical sampling algorithms that might need a polynomial time for big data. The paper contains a quantum big data algorithm which relies on nonsparse matrix exponentiation of a small matrix which contains the inverse of the training data inner-product (kernel) matrix. The matrix inversion is done efficiently, that is by linear complexity.
